column65:
I want to preface this by saying everything about the clan has been improving over the years and this is the best version of EG we have ever had. 

That said, changes are incredibly slow and incremental, in part because it all seems reliant on one or two people doing everything. There are sections of the clan, like mentoring and other games, that feel abandoned and lost. Despite a punishment system, some people get instantly kicked for things others say without any discipline. I think the burnout of some staff members is apparent and there doesn't seem to be proper support for them or a backup plan. It is unclear what certain leaders actually do to keep things running smoothly and it seems grossly unfair for Lexi to consistently have to pull a disproportionate amount of weight. 

In other industries there is this idea of 'How would your project/company run if someone critical got hit by a bus?' If something happened and forced some people to step down, I'm not confident the clan would be ok.

column15:
I think this could be a hot take, but the feeling I get is that people are too comfortable within their own friends groups within EG. My few points to rise on this are the increasing amount of people asking to be matched with XYZ during the bigger events  (e.g GR) and the lack of interaction with the #team-search channel. This is obviously anecdotical, but I've made most of my connections through events and the events really do feel different when you're amongst a group of friends who don't communicate much with the team's chat/toss invite and suggestion to others and all that. 

As a day-to-day, the social atmosphere of the Discord seems pretty active with the regular voicers and chatters, but as someone who's not part of these group, it's pretty hard to find a place to fit in if you're not a loud voice as there's a lot of inside jokes/banters or simply just a lot of people at once in a VC! Might just be a me skill issue but that do be how I feel.
column16:
I'd define EG has a very versatile clan with irons, GIMs, UIM, normies and GMs, we pretty much have it all with events to which almost any account of any progress will find a chance to shine. I'd tell anyone who wants to join that they'll find themselves a home with a lot of great people, however if they want to find a group for certain activities, from my own experience, they'd have better chances by keeping an eye out in-game or by VC activities than the discord role shootouts. 

Albeit hectic and crazy at time in the channels or in-game chat, EG is still very well organized and know where to draw the line between fun and seriousness.

column18:
Tbow learner raid spoon with Cremmy obviously? 

On a more serious note, I will touch a sensitive cord and say the post-Ion era. At the time I was still only small gem and had seen great things from the clan (Hexchange!) and felt like EG was such an empowerment of my enjoyment towards the game, that I was afraid of a leadership dramatic event would slowly crash and burn the whole thing, just like it did for so many clans in the past. 

However hard it was for everyone in the behind-the-scenes and what I know from back then, I found the transition incredibly smooth and things only got better from there. This moment stuck to me, because it showed the hardship that all of the staff goes through to keep things straight for our gaming home and that we're not free from negative events from happening, but we're much more mature than many of the other clans as to be able to ride the tide and stand tall at the end of it all.

It just doesn't, albeit I've not been part of many. In OSRS I've had 3 or 4 different experience. 2 of them were very elitist, borderline trying to play around Jagex's ToS  (tiptoeing on "you need inferno to be in the clan and if you don't have it we can do something about it wink wink" kinda thing) and the others I was in were a bit more diverse and friendly like EG, however there was not much of anything to get attached to. There was the lack of events to keep people coming, getting to know new people, trying new content and being motivated to work towards a goal as a team and it slowly built into a very clique-based clan which imploded upon the first drama event between co-leaders. 

EG is simply much more well put together, glued as a unit and respectable of their own and everyone's time and effort than anything I've seen.

For example, we had CoX CM during GR. column65:
I'd love to see more recurrent "Group-Up" flash event where clanmates are encouraged to come together and do things. I could be led by mentors for lesser experienced members and be done in smaller groups rather than masses we've seen. E.g I'd love to see these events encourage people to run trios TOA rather than form an 8-man party as to differentiate the "group-up" aspect vs Mass and maybe encourage less experienced players to have a real taste of the content and come learn, smaller CoX teams rather than masses to better maybe even a scaled raid learning for the higher gamers I dunno! It doesn't have to be raids either, could be a monthly small team nex/gwd where it's trying to make small groups and there's mentors for the less experienced players to group up with... 

I'd basically just love to see more content aimed towards inclusiveness, trying to form smaller groups and maybe push less experienced players towards new content and also encourage the less active/more shy players to join smaller groups.

column67:
A better PVM-Mentor structure and initiative, I'd love to see PVM Mentor actively run initiatives rather than sit for a ticket to be created. I'd love for Ranked members to be held accountable for their role, for mentors to be more than just helping out a reaching hand but also hosting learners event and giving opportunities more than a mass. 

I'd love to see more visibility on what the clan leadership is dealing with so we feel more invested and included in the whole processes as well as better see the meaning behind the roles. 

Hope it helps love y'all

column24: From a member’s perspective, the answer would be yes; however, from a staff standpoint, it would be no. Recently, there has been a growing concern that when issues are raised, discussions are often shut down with the assurance that leadership will handle them—yet no follow-up or outreach is made to the individual involved. This has created an environment where individuals feel hesitant to speak openly, out of concern for potential retaliation, such as demotion or even removal from the group.
column25: I would like to see leadership place greater trust in lower-ranked staff members by empowering them to handle situations more independently. Additionally, it would be beneficial for leaders to more consistently follow up on how situations or tickets are managed, providing active feedback. This kind of engagement would help staff continue to grow, improve their decision-making, and strengthen overall team effectiveness.
column26: Yes, I do feel that my voice matters in how the clan is run. When communication is open and feedback is acknowledged, it creates a sense of inclusion and shared purpose. It reinforces the idea that everyone plays a role in shaping the clan’s direction and overall success.

column14: Coming from a hardcore background in games like WoW and Starcraft 2, I was looking for a chill, friendly environment without the pressure of mandatory attendance. This clan strikes the perfect balance; I can stay focused on my personal goals while having the freedom to choose which events I want to attend.
column15: While my own anxiety sometimes keeps me from being the most active chatter, I can clearly see how social and welcoming this clan is. It’s easy to get along with people here because the community lacks the toxicity you’d typically find in WoW Mythic+ or League of Legends ranked play. It feels like a safe, low-pressure space to engage whenever I’m ready
column17: While I find joining new groups to be a daunting experience, my time here has been different. It has gradually become much easier for me to engage in-game and sign up for events, as the clan's friendly nature helped lower the pressure I usually feel.
column18: The Sailing launch and the 'Skill of the Week' event are my favorite memories so far. It reminded me of the excitement of a new WoW expansion launch—everyone was active, helpful, and working toward a goal together, which made the experience much more enjoyable.
column19: To be honest, I’m still finding my spot. Because of my anxiety and autism, small talk doesn't come easily to me, and I’m not always the most active in chat. However, I’m hopeful that as I start learning to raid with the clan, those shared experiences will make it easier to build deeper connections and find my place in the community.
column20: "This is my first RuneScape clan, but compared to my history in WoW, it’s a much better experience. I spent years in high-ranking world-race guilds where the environment was often elitist or clique-heavy. While I had the skill to keep up, the toxicity eventually made the game unenjoyable. Since 2012, I’ve prioritized communities like this one—places that are laid-back and progress-oriented without the elitism. Even in a more relaxed setting, I still maintain the same high personal standards for my own gameplay as I did in top-tier guilds, but I prefer an environment where mistakes aren't met with anger or frustration toward others.
column21: Since I’m still relatively new and split my time with WoW, I haven't noticed any features fading away. However, I really enjoyed the recent Nex mass that happened outside of the official schedule. It’s the kind of content that really highlights the value of a clan, especially for those of us who don't have a dedicated small team yet
column22: A really good week would look like transitioning into higher-level PvM with the community. Specifically, I’m looking forward to learning Theatre of Blood and CoX. Having that structured environment to master the mechanics of ToB would feel like a great success, both as a player and as a member of the clan.
column23: A common misunderstanding for hardcore newcomers might be our approach to mistakes. Someone coming from a strictly competitive background might expect a 'win-at-all-costs' attitude and feel frustrated during events. They might not realize at first that our priority is maintaining a friendly, inclusive atmosphere where it's okay to learn and fail without being met with toxicity
column24: In my experience, the leadership has been perfectly fine and approachable when we've chatted. However, due to my personal anxiety, I often struggle to reach out because I worry about 'bothering' someone. While the staff hasn't done anything to cause this feeling, it still makes DMs or PMs feel like a hurdle. Knowing there are structured features to use helps, but I'm still working on feeling comfortable enough to use them.
column25: The staff does a great job, but the variety of roles (Coordinators, Mods, Leaders, etc.) can make it confusing to know who to approach. As someone who struggles with anxiety, I often spend a lot of time researching or looking things up myself first because I want to be sure I’m speaking to the correct person and don't want to come across as 'clueless.' A simplified 'Who to Contact' guide would really help reduce that social pressure and make the leadership feel more accessible without the fear of bothering the wrong person.
column26: I’m still relatively new, but I feel like my voice matters because of the transparent way the clan is run. Even though I tend to stay quiet in chat, seeing these regular check-ins and surveys makes it clear that the leadership values the input of the entire community, not just the most vocal or long-term members.
column29: When I had a problem or question
column31: I really appreciate how the staff has cultivated an environment that feels safe for players with different social needs. Coming from high-pressure WoW and League environments, it’s refreshing to have a leadership team that prioritizes a 'toxic-free' culture. Even though I struggle with the social side of things due to my anxiety, I can see the effort they put into making this a welcoming place for everyone.
column32: I particularly enjoy Skill of the Week because it is a solo competition. Since I have high-level commitments in my WoW guild, I prefer solo events where I can push myself at my own pace. With team-focused events like Bingo, I sometimes hesitate to join because I don't want to feel like a burden if I can't give 100% effort due to my schedule in other games.
column33: I’d love to see more events designed to push people out of their comfort zones, specifically for high-stakes content like Theatre of Blood. Because failure in ToB can result in a wipe for the entire team, it feels very daunting to try. It would be great to have 'No-Pressure Learning Nights' or events that explicitly encourage participation without the fear of failure, helping members bridge the gap between mid-level and elite PvM.
column34: It’s hard to find any faults in how events are currently run, as they are very well-organized. My only suggestion for improvement would be what I mentioned previously: creating more 'safe-to-fail' environments for higher-stakes content like ToB. Aside from that, the current system works great for my schedule
column35: The events themselves are great, but my participation is often limited by my WoW schedule. When I’m in the middle of world-rank progression or PTR testing, I can’t always commit the time needed for team-based clan events. It’s a personal scheduling conflict rather than a problem with the event styles; I just prefer not to join a team if I can't give them my full focus.
column36: My highlight so far has been the Sailing launch Skill of the Week. I’m currently participating in my first Bingo, but the SOTW format really worked for me. It felt like a great way to celebrate a new skill launch with the community while still maintaining the 'play at your own pace' style that fits my current schedule.
column37: I tend to feel lukewarm about team events like Bingo. It’s not because of the event itself, but because I worry about being perceived as someone who isn't putting in the effort. If my WoW raid schedule or PTR testing means I have to miss a few days, I feel like I'm not giving 100%, and I’d rather sit out than be a teammate who isn't fully 'all-in
column38: In terms of stats, the events fit my account well. However, I personally feel like I’m not 'at that level' yet because I’m not a reliable raider for Theatre of Blood or Chambers of Xeric (CM). Because I have very high expectations for my own gameplay, I hesitate to join high-level team events until I feel I can contribute at 100% without being a potential burden to the group.
column39: Discord pings in the events channel are my primary way of staying informed. I tend to overlook the calendar, so having that direct notification is much more effective for me. It allows me to see immediately if an event fits into my current schedule without me having to go searching for the information.
column40: I definitely feel anxiety around showing up, but it’s mostly driven by my own high expectations. I have a personal shortcoming where I feel like I need to be 'decent' or 'good' at everything before I even start. Because I don't want to feel unprepared or like a burden to the group, I often set the bar for myself much higher than what is actually required for the event.
column41: My main conflict is the timing of WoW progression. While my guild clears content very quickly—usually down to a 2-hour clear on Wednesdays and Sundays—the initial PTR testing and Live progression stages require a full-week commitment. Once we have the raids on farm, I have plenty of time for clan events, but during those 'World Rank' push windows, I have to prioritize my raid team.
column42: I think the variety is fine, as the Bingo and Skill of the Week formats are handled well and stay engaging. However, I’d love to see more events that push people toward specific mechanical challenges, like forced group content for Theatre of Blood or Combat Achievement pushes. It would be a fun way to encourage progress and help members learn difficult content together in a structured, low-pressure environment.
column43: I’ve thought about an event that focuses specifically on the three raids—ToB, ToA, and CoX—along with group bosses like Yama. It would be great to have a week dedicated to either getting initial kills for learners or pushing for the harder Challenge/Contract versions for more experienced players. Having a structured clan event for these would make the high-stakes team environment feel much more accessible.
column44: I usually feel energized and happy after an event wraps up. They provide a much-needed break from the monotony of long solo grinds, like the current Skilling Bingo. It’s refreshing to have a specific goal to work toward alongside the clan, and it makes the overall OSRS experience feel less like a repetitive task and more like a shared activity.
column45: For me, the fun turns into an obligation when I feel like I'm letting a team down by not being online. I tend to avoid team events for this reason. However, if there was a 'Casual' or 'Flexible' team bracket for players who can't commit to a full-week grind, I would definitely join. It would remove the anxiety of feeling like a burden while still allowing me to contribute when I have the time.
column46: The communication is already quite good. The Discord pings are the most effective way for me to stay informed since I rarely check the calendar manually. As long as those stay consistent, the process is smooth enough for me to plan around my other gaming commitments in WoW.
column47: My biggest hurdle is the fear of causing a wipe or being the reason the team fails. Coming from a high-level WoW background, I’m used to a 'zero-mistake' environment, and I worry that others will get frustrated if I fail a mechanic in ToB or CoX. Because of this, I tend to 'over-prepare' solo—like pushing myself to a 300 Solo ToA with Insanity—before I ever feel 'worthy' of joining a team. I understand wipes are part of learning, but I have a hard time shaking the expectation that I should be perfect from the start.
column48: I think the pace is fine, but as a social clan, we could do more to 'nudge' people toward higher-level content like Theatre of Blood or Nex. While the PvM learning applications are great, they can feel a bit formal. I’d love to see dedicated 'Social Raid Days' where the goal isn't just a perfect clear, but a chill environment for people to learn the mechanics together without the pressure of feeling like a burden.
column49: I personally struggle with this because asking a 'dumb' question can make you feel like you look stupid to the group. It feels like people who stay quiet and then make a mistake are often judged less harshly than someone who admits they don't know something upfront. However, from my time raiding in WoW, I’ve realized that it’s actually more responsible to ask the question than to stay silent and cause a wipe. I’m still looking for that 'safe' space in the clan where asking a basic question isn't looked down upon.
column50: The clan is welcoming, but the 'Required Gear' meta is intimidating. I’m used to WoW progression where you clear high-level raids in last-season gear because you're skilled enough to handle the mechanics. In OSRS, it feels like there’s a 'Barrier to Entry' based on items rather than ability. I’d love to see more groups that focus on learning content with 'Mid-Tier' gear (like Bandos for ToB), proving that you don't need a multi-billion GP setup to be a reliable raider.
column51: Theatre of Blood. I’ve already pushed myself to master 300 Solo ToA with Insanity, so I know I have the mechanical floor for it. I’ve just been held back by the anxiety of finding a team that is okay with the initial learning wipes. With clan support, I’d love to finally bridge that gap from solo mastery to team progression.
column52: I find it hard to tell. In a social clan, there’s always a risk that being highly competitive or focused on progression can be misinterpreted as elitism. I try to balance my desire to be a top-tier raider with the clan’s social nature, so I don’t always seek out notice for my progress. I’d rather be seen as a reliable teammate than someone who is just trying to outshine others.
column53: I feel most like part of the clan when I’m actually contributing to a team’s success. column54: Currently, I haven't found a specific group to casually hang out with yet. column55: I feel less connected to the PvM side of the clan, specifically when it comes to team raids and group bosses. Perception is reality. It is a phrase I live by, and believe in. Unfortunately, you are all in situations where you are in the light of everything you say and do. 

No one is ever asking you to not be human, be a robot, or anything of that nature.

But... things can be taken differently than they may have been meant, even if sometimes it is as simple as an emote. I just think it is important to reflect on the emotions that are typed in public channels, it is hard as humans to set those to the side, even if they are entirely external factors! Step away for 30 minutes if needed, reread before you hit send. We are all human, and I do not believe anyones status or skill level makes you higher, especially when we are talking about a community for escape inside a non-competitive game :) 

Always bring up, never down.
column26:
Yes and no...? I feel like I have the outlet to make a suggestion whenever needed, but to my knowledge there is never been outward voting / polls for perceptions regarding larger ticket items in the clan. 

I do greatly appreciate all the surveys that come, but it can be hard to see what is being actively listened to / applied from those! 

Only worry ever from the clan is just not letting ego circumvent the community and nature you have all built. If you want a sweaty ass clan, then there are those available. Pushing people to learn is amazing and encouraged, but not everyone has the capacity, mental energy, or time to go the distance. *que the music*

Honestly, I am sure you have had suggestions and have tried many things regarding balance, maybe even some of these things below but... regarding balance:

Have you thought of doing an ask of those who signup how much they are doing to play? There are many times I am very busy with work, and other times I am not. This massively changes the teammate I am, and the balance that would and should go into placing that person on a team. 

Have you ever thought of doing a light, completely hidden from the public, "elo" system for each member? Basically, have a spreadsheet to track your players, I would assume it is hard to track each player (players leave and join, become better, etc.) in that regard. Have some sort of numerical values (PvM, Skilling, average time played or actual time surveyed they will play) for each player that could lead to placing members and teams more balanced.

Also, it is okay to break apart people / not take requests for some events (in situations, probably encouraged, helps new teams form!) I think we are starting to see some of the "samey" teams from events now with all the requests/friend groups wanting to stay together

column65:
EG has a genuinely special community — the culture here is something I haven’t found in other clans, and the friendships formed are real ones that extend beyond the game.
That said, there are some structural areas worth addressing:
Leadership: Four leaders feels like more than necessary. Soul’s absence and general unavailability make it hard to justify the role, and his approach doesn’t seem well-suited to leadership responsibilities. Kiuyu brings some strengths, but his volatility and confrontational attitude — particularly during events — reflect poorly on the clan. Both would likely be more effective in different capacities. Lemon is a strong and valuable part of the team. If a third leader is ever considered for checks and balances purposes, it should be someone who genuinely embodies what makes this community good.

PvM Mentors: Gigi stands out as the only mentor who is consistently and actively mentoring. Crem and Nuc are largely limited to Cox and aren’t especially visible. The mentorship system as a whole is underutilized and has seen a lot of turnover — which speaks to how much room for improvement exists. Giving Gigi ownership over this area and the freedom to reshape it could unlock real value. PvM mentors running raid nights, progression events, and sustained programming (not one-off attempts like trying Tob Tuesdays once or twice and giving up) would make a meaningful difference.

Staff: Staff visibility matters. Some moderators have minimal event participation — in one case, none since receiving the role. Events are the social backbone of the clan, and staff should be present in them. There’s also a perception issue worth noting: when staff primarily interact with each other rather than the broader membership, it can read as cliquish, which works against the welcoming culture EG works hard to build.


Overall, the culture that’s been cultivated at EG is the biggest strength we have (out of many!) — and that is worth protecting and leaning into.
